[1] Asthma – Emergency Treatment Options
In emergency situations involving acute asthma exacerbations presenting with signs such as wheezing, difficulty breathing, chest tightness, or cyanosis, administration of nebulized albuterol (salbutamol) is considered first-line therapy according to national guidelines including those published by WHO and major healthcare institutions worldwide. Nebulized delivery ensures efficient lung deposition and rapid symptom improvement within minutes after initiation.[2]
Emergency department providers follow standardized algorithms incorporating prompt evaluation alongside immediate pharmacologic intervention tailored specifically for severity grading scales used internationally (such as GINA or GOLD).[2]
When administered promptly during moderate-to-severe episodes accompanied by hypoxemia or impending respiratory failure, intravenous corticosteroids like methylprednisolone may be added shortly thereafter as adjunctive therapy aiming at decreasing ongoing inflammation.[2]
However, initiating IV steroids immediately without confirming diagnosis or ruling out alternative causes may lead to unnecessary exposure and adverse reactions such as hyperglycemia or fluid retention.[2]
Therefore careful triaging based on clinical presentation determines timing and route selection for steroid administration—avoiding prophylactic dosing unless indicated medically necessary under strict oversight frameworks defined by institutional policies.[2]
[2] Asthma – First-Line Treatments During Exacerbations
First-line treatment during acute asthma exacerbations consists primarily of delivering fast-acting beta-adrenergic receptor stimulators such as salbutamol via inhalation routes including metered dose inhalers (MDIs) or nebulizers,[2] especially when patients exhibit clear indicators suggestive of reversible airflow obstruction confirmed through spirometry testing conducted off-label during emergencies due to practicality concerns faced by frontline personnel dealing with urgent cases.[2]
These medications act quickly—within minutes—to relieve bronchoconstriction by stimulating beta-2 receptors located abundantly in pulmonary smooth muscle cells leading directly to relaxation and widening of airways facilitating easier gas exchange.[2]
They are safe for repeated use during crisis periods although caution should still be exercised considering potential side effects including tachycardia or tremors arising from stimulation-induced sympathetic activation.[2]
Second-line interventions may include systemic glucocorticoids delivered parenterally (intravenously or intramuscularly) when severe exacerbations progress beyond what can be managed with local bronchodilators alone—or when oxygen saturation drops below acceptable thresholds necessitating supplemental oxygen provision simultaneously.[2]
Systemic steroids help suppress inflammatory mediators involved in asthmatic responses over hours-long durations offering longer-term stabilization compared with brief symptomatic relief offered initially by beta agonists.[2]
In contrast, drugs like aspirin inhibit prostacyclin synthesis potentially worsening asthma symptoms especially in patients exhibiting aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD);
Answer: A
